chunk_summary: This section discusses synovial fluid analysis and inflammatory marker thresholds for diagnosing pediatric septic arthritis, emphasizing cell counts, neutrophil percentages, and adjunctive markers like CRP and ESR.
learning_objectives: Understand the diagnostic role of synovial fluid analysis in suspected septic arthritis.
Interpret synovial white cell counts and neutrophil percentages in the context of bacterial arthritis.
Recognize the importance of inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR) in guiding joint aspiration decisions.
Incorporate clinical probability and laboratory findings for a comprehensive diagnostic approach.
key_facts: Synovial white cell counts >50,000 cells/mm³ strongly favor bacterial arthritis, but counts of 25,000–50,000 can still indicate infection.
Neutrophil predominance >75% in synovial fluid is highly suggestive of septic arthritis.
CRP >20 mg/L or ESR >20 mm/hr should lower the threshold for joint aspiration in symptomatic children.
Kingella kingae infections in toddlers may present with lower synovial cell counts and inflammatory markers compared to staphylococcal infections.
Clinical probability, synovial cell count, neutrophil percentage, Gram stain, and serial CRP trends should be combined for diagnosis.
reference_quote: A synovial white cell count above about 50,000 cells per cubic millimeter strongly favors bacterial arthritis, but lower counts do not exclude infection. (Source: PIDS/IDSA 2023 Guidelines for Diagnosing and Managing Acute Bacterial Arthritis in Pediatrics)
document_key_topics: Diagnostic approach for pediatric septic arthritis
Role of Kingella kingae in atypical presentations
